notes.title: Reflection of Light by Spherical Mirrors
points: Sign Convention: Cartesian sign convention is used. Pole (P) is the origin. Direction of incident light is positive x-axis.
Focal Length (f): Distance between Pole and Principal Focus. For spherical mirrors, f = R/2.
Concave Mirror: Converging mirror. Real focus.
Convex Mirror: Diverging mirror. Virtual focus.

notes.label: Mirror Formula
conditions: u is object distance (always negative).
v is image distance.
f is focal length (negative for concave, positive for convex).

notes.label: Linear Magnification (Mirror)
conditions: m is negative for real, inverted images.
m is positive for virtual, erect images.

notes.title: Refraction of Light
points: Bending of light when passing obliquely from one medium to another.
Snell's Law: Ratio of sine of angle of incidence to sine of angle of refraction is constant.

notes.label: Snell's Law
conditions: n21 is refractive index of medium 2 with respect to medium 1.
n is absolute refractive index (c/v).

notes.title: Refraction through Glass Slab
notes.caption: Emergent ray is parallel to incident ray but laterally displaced.

notes.title: Total Internal Reflection (TIR)
points: Occurs when light travels from denser to rarer medium.
If angle of incidence i > critical angle ic, the ray is totally reflected back into the denser medium.
Applications: Mirage, Diamond brilliance, Prisms, Optical Fibres.

notes.label: Critical Angle Condition
conditions: n1 is rarer medium (often air, n=1), n2 is denser medium.

notes.title: Total Internal Reflection
notes.caption: Ray undergoes reflection when incidence angle exceeds critical angle ic.

notes.title: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces
points: Refraction occurs at a single spherical interface separating two media.

notes.label: Refraction Formula (Spherical Surface)
conditions: Light travels from medium n1 to n2.
R is radius of curvature.

notes.title: Refraction at Spherical Surface
notes.caption: Geometry for deriving the relationship between u, v, n1, n2, and R.

notes.title: Refraction by Lenses
points: A lens is a transparent medium bounded by two spherical surfaces.
Thin lens approximation: Thickness is negligible compared to radii of curvature.

notes.label: Lens Maker's Formula
conditions: R1 and R2 are radii of curvature of the two surfaces.
Sign convention applies to R1 and R2.

notes.label: Thin Lens Formula
conditions: Same form for convex and concave lenses.

notes.label: Magnification (Lens)
conditions: m is positive for virtual image, negative for real image.

notes.title: Power of a Lens
points: Measure of the convergence or divergence which a lens introduces in the light falling on it.
SI Unit: Dioptre (D). 1 D = 1 m^-1.

notes.label: Power of Lens
conditions: f must be in meters.
P is positive for convex, negative for concave.

notes.title: Combination of Lenses
points: When two thin lenses are in contact, the combination acts as a single lens.
Total magnification is the product of individual magnifications.

notes.label: Lenses in Contact
conditions: Algebraic sum (signs must be included).

notes.title: Refraction through a Prism
points: Light bends towards the base of the prism.
Angle of deviation (delta) depends on angle of incidence.
At minimum deviation, the refracted ray inside the prism is parallel to the base.

notes.label: Prism Equation
conditions: A is angle of prism, delta is deviation, i is incidence, e is emergence.

notes.label: Refractive Index of Prism Material
conditions: Valid only at condition of minimum deviation (delta_m).
At minimum deviation, i = e and r1 = r2.

notes.title: Refraction through Prism
notes.caption: Path of ray showing angle of deviation delta.

notes.title: Graph of Deviation vs Incidence Angle
notes.caption: Curve shows a unique minimum value of deviation delta_m.

notes.title: Dispersion and Scattering
points: Dispersion: Splitting of white light into constituent colours (VIBGYOR) because refractive index depends on wavelength (Cauchy's formula).
Scattering: Change in direction of light by irregularities in medium (Rayleigh Scattering).
Condition for Rayleigh scattering: Size of scatterer a << wavelength lambda.

notes.label: Rayleigh Scattering Law
conditions: Shorter wavelengths (Blue) scatter much more than longer wavelengths (Red).
Explains blue sky and reddish sunrise/sunset.

notes.title: The Eye
points: Ciliary muscles adjust the curvature of the lens to focus objects at different distances (Accommodation).
Near point (D) = 25 cm. Far point = Infinity.

notes.title: Simple Microscope
points: A convex lens of short focal length.
Object placed between focus and optical center.

notes.label: Magnification (Simple Microscope)
conditions: Image formed at Near Point (D = 25 cm).

notes.title: Compound Microscope
points: Consists of two convex lenses: Objective (short f, small aperture) and Eyepiece (larger f, larger aperture).
Objective forms a real, inverted, magnified image.
Eyepiece acts as a simple microscope to magnify this intermediate image.

notes.title: Compound Microscope
notes.caption: Ray diagram showing image formation by objective and eyepiece.

notes.label: Magnification (Compound Microscope)
conditions: L is tube length (distance between second focus of objective and first focus of eyepiece).
Image at near point.

notes.title: Refracting Telescope
points: Used for observing distant objects.
Objective: Large focal length and large aperture (to gather light).
Eyepiece: Small focal length.

notes.title: Refracting Telescope
notes.caption: Parallel rays from infinity focused by objective, viewed through eyepiece.

notes.label: Magnification (Telescope - Normal Adjustment)
conditions: Final image formed at infinity.
Length of telescope L = fo + fe.

notes.title: Reflecting Telescope (Cassegrain)
points: Uses a parabolic concave mirror as the objective.
Advantages: No chromatic aberration, no spherical aberration (if parabolic), lighter weight, easier to support.

notes.title: Cassegrain Reflecting Telescope
notes.caption: Primary concave mirror and secondary convex mirror focus light to the eye.

ncert_golden_lines: Light travels with a speed of 3x10^8 m/s in vacuum.
A convex mirror always produces a virtual image independent of the location of the object.
The refractive index of a medium depends on the wavelength of light.
Total internal reflection occurs only when light travels from a denser medium to a rarer medium.
The power of a lens is the reciprocal of its focal length in meters.
Blue light is scattered more than red light.
The largest telescope in the world is a reflecting telescope.

diagram_descriptors.detailed_description: Emergent ray is parallel to incident ray but laterally displaced.
Ray undergoes reflection when incidence angle exceeds critical angle ic.
Geometry for deriving the relationship between u, v, n1, n2, and R.
Path of ray showing angle of deviation delta.
